meta.title: strftime — date format builder
meta.description: Interactive strftime cheat sheet and format builder: live preview, plain-language explanations, presets and C / Python / Ruby compatibility.

hero.title: Build your strftime format
hero.subtitle: Click directives, reorder chips, or type freely. Live preview, plain-language explanations, and C / Python / Ruby support at a glance.

builder.label: Format string
builder.empty: Pick a preset, tap directives in the palette, or type a format here.
builder.srInstructions: To pick up a chip, press space or enter. Use the arrow keys to move it, space or enter to drop it, and escape to cancel.

chip.literalNoPercent: For a literal % sign, use the %% directive instead.

reference.title: Reference
reference.subtitle: Every directive, its live output for the preview date, and where it works.

modifiers.title: Flags and width
modifiers.intro: Between the % and the letter, glibc and Ruby accept modifiers that tweak padding and case:
modifiers.underscore: pad with spaces instead of zeros
modifiers.hash: uppercase the name directives (%a %A %b %B %h); lowercase %p and %Z
modifiers.widthText: a number sets the minimum field width
modifiers.colon: colons only apply to %z for the +hh:mm offset forms
modifiers.python: Python documents none of these flags: they may happen to work through the platform C library, but they are not portable.

command.placeholder: Type a code, a name or a description...
command.hint: Press Enter to add to the format

footer.pitch: Your perfect strftime format: live explanations, presets and dialects.

notFound.title: Unknown directive
notFound.description: This page isn't part of any format string. Let's head back to the builder.

Y.description: Year with century, as a decimal number.
Y.notes: The backbone of sortable dates, with %m and %d. For ISO week dates, pair %V with %G instead.

y.description: Year without century, zero-padded to two digits.
y.notes: Ambiguous by design: 26 could be 1926 or 2026. Prefer %Y anywhere durability matters.

C.description: Century: the year divided by 100, truncated, zero-padded.
C.notes: Combine with %y to rebuild a full year. Absent from the Python format table.

G.notes: Differs from %Y around January 1st when the ISO week belongs to the neighboring year. Use it with %V and %u, never with %m or %d.

g.description: ISO week-based year without century, two digits.
g.notes: Two-digit variant of %G. Absent from the Python format table.

m.description: Month as a zero-padded two-digit number.
m.notes: Use %-m in glibc and Ruby to drop the leading zero.

B.description: Full month name, according to the locale.
B.notes: Locale-dependent: try switching the preview locale. %^B uppercases the whole name in glibc and Ruby.

b.description: Abbreviated month name, according to the locale.
b.notes: %h is an exact synonym in glibc and Ruby.

h.notes: Kept for SUSv2 compatibility; prefer %b. Absent from the Python format table.

V.description: ISO 8601 week number: week 01 is the one containing the first Thursday of the year.
V.notes: The standard week number in Europe. Pair it with %G instead of %Y to avoid bugs around New Year.

U.description: Week number, where week 01 starts at the first Sunday of the year.
U.notes: Days before the first Sunday fall in week 00. The American convention.

W.description: Week number, where week 01 starts at the first Monday of the year.
W.notes: Like %U with Monday weeks: days before the first Monday fall in week 00.

d.notes: Use %e for space padding, or %-d in glibc and Ruby for no padding.

e.notes: Single digits render with a leading space, like ' 5'. Absent from the Python format table.

j.description: Day of the year, zero-padded to three digits.
j.notes: Goes from 001 to 366. Handy for log rotation and julian-style stamps.

A.description: Full weekday name, according to the locale.
A.notes: Locale-dependent. %^A gives FRIDAY in glibc and Ruby.

a.description: Abbreviated weekday name, according to the locale.
a.notes: Opens RFC 2822 dates, as in 'Fri, 12 Jun 2026'.

u.description: Weekday as a number, Monday is 1 (ISO 8601).
u.notes: Runs 1..7 with Sunday as 7. Compare %w, where Sunday is 0.

w.description: Weekday as a number, Sunday is 0.
w.notes: Runs 0..6. Compare %u, where Monday is 1 and Sunday is 7.

H.notes: Use %k for space padding in glibc and Ruby.

I.notes: Always pair it with %p or %P, otherwise 02 could be night or afternoon.

k.notes: GNU extension in C, standard in Ruby, absent from the Python table. ' 8' instead of '08'.

l.notes: GNU extension in C, standard in Ruby, absent from the Python table.

p.description: Uppercase meridian indicator, AM or PM.
p.notes: Noon is PM, midnight is AM. Locale-dependent, and empty in some locales.

P.description: Lowercase meridian indicator, am or pm.
P.notes: GNU extension with the case inverted versus %p. Standard in Ruby, absent from Python.

M.notes: Runs 00..59, the one directive nobody argues about.

S.notes: glibc allows 60 for leap seconds; Python and Ruby stop at 59.

L.description: Millisecond of the second, three digits.
L.notes: Ruby only. Python uses %f (microseconds); C has no sub-second directive at all.

N.description: Fractional seconds, where the width picks the precision.
N.notes: Ruby only. %3N is milliseconds, %6N microseconds, %9N nanoseconds (the default).

f.notes: Python only, implemented by datetime itself. The Ruby equivalent is %6N.

z.notes: The RFC 2822 form. For the ISO 8601 colon form, see %:z.

zColon.description: UTC offset with a colon, like +02:00.
zColon.notes: The ISO 8601 form. Ruby, and Python since 3.12; glibc does not implement it.

Z.notes: Output depends on the platform and locale (CEST, GMT+2...). Fine to display, risky to parse.

s.description: Seconds since the Unix epoch, 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC.
s.notes: The universal machine timestamp. In Python, use timestamp() instead.

F.notes: The sortable date. Absent from the Python format table.

D.notes: Ambiguous outside the US: 06/12 reads as December 6th in most of the world. Prefer %F.

T.notes: Same output as %X in the C locale.

r.notes: Locale-dependent in glibc; some locales render it empty.

c.description: The locale's full date and time representation.
c.notes: In the C locale: %a %b %e %H:%M:%S %Y. Try switching the preview locale to see it change.

x.description: The locale's date representation, without the time.

X.description: The locale's time representation, without the date.

plus.notes: Ruby only, mirroring the BSD date command output. Supported by Date#strftime and DateTime#strftime, but not Time#strftime.

n.notes: Absent from the Python format table; type a real newline there instead.

t.notes: Absent from the Python format table.

percent.notes: The only way to output % itself.
